items.q: Why does increasing the intensity of light below the threshold frequency not eject electrons?
items.correct: Each photon's energy is below the work function; many low-energy photons can't combine to eject an electron.
items.misconception: Enough low-frequency photons should eventually eject an electron if you wait long enough.
items.random_wrong: Low-frequency light doesn't have enough mass to push electrons out of the metal.
items.why: Students think energy is cumulative (more photons = more energy delivered), missing that each electron interacts with one photon at a time in the photoelectric effect.

items.q: When you rub a balloon and stick it to the wall, what has been transferred?
items.correct: Electrons have been transferred between the balloon and the cloth (not the wall).
items.misconception: Protons jumped from the balloon to the wall, causing static cling.
items.random_wrong: Charge was created by friction and pulled the balloon to the wall.
items.why: Students don't know that nuclei (and protons) are essentially immobile in everyday situations — only electrons move in static electricity.

items.q: What distinguishes gamma rays from X-rays of the same frequency?
items.correct: Origin — gamma rays come from nuclear processes; X-rays come from electron transitions or deceleration of charged particles.
items.misconception: Gamma rays have higher frequency than any X-ray.
items.random_wrong: Gamma rays are slower than X-rays.
items.why: Students distinguish bands by frequency exclusively, missing that gamma and X-ray bands overlap in frequency and are classified by origin.

items.q: Which best describes the second law of thermodynamics?
items.correct: The total entropy of an isolated system never decreases.
items.misconception: Energy is conserved in any process.
items.random_wrong: Heat flows spontaneously from cold to hot.
items.why: It's The total entropy of an isolated system never decreases.. “Energy is conserved in any process.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A heat engine operates between 500 K and 300 K. Its maximum theoretical efficiency is:
items.why: Correct answer: 40%. “20%” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
